item.title: Docker Hardened Images: crafted by humans, protected by AI
item.author: Christian Dupuis
item.content:encoded: At Docker, we are building our hardened images to exacting standards. That means carefully crafting by hand, because humans are still the best security architects. That said, we understand the value of AI and deploy it as an extra set of eyes at critical junctures in our Docker Hardened Image (DHI) build process. With this dual approach, our users get better coverage and more secure products. Humans aren’t perfect, but AI can help them become better. Here’s a recent example of how the AI tools we use for DHI caught a bug, made a product more secure, and contributed back to the community.How the AI guardrail stepped inOur upstream release automation opened a routine version bump for nginx-exporter. As part of our release process, the DHI AI guardrail fetched the upstream diffs that the bump would pull in and scanned them with language-aware checks. It spotted a classic logic inversion in the exporter’s new proxy-protocol path and immediately blocked our PR from auto-merging. I reproduced the issue, confirmed the diagnosis, and submitted a small fix upstream. Because the guardrail held the line, customers never saw the bug.That sequence matters. A normal dependency update would have sailed through and shipped a regression. Instead, Docker’s AI guardrail acted like a release bouncer at the door of a bar. The AI guardrail looked at what was about to enter, recognized a risk pattern, and stopped it from coming in and causing trouble. A human Docker engineer then read the flagged issue, proved the failure and sent the fix. The fix was then accepted by the project, improving their code base. So, this fix not only improved DHI but also improved the project for everyone. While the fix pull request has been reviewed and approved by the upstream maintainers, our DHI build pipeline applied the patch and shipped the new, patched version to our customers. Making sure that customers never saw the regression in their environments. The AI assisted DHI processIronically, standard AI coding assistants didn’t spot the flaw. This is why having our own internal AI guardrails is so critical. They provide the extra layer of support and the specialization that is hard to get from general-purpose coding assistants.This is how we want AI to show up in our pipeline. It is not a replacement for engineering judgment, but a force multiplier for safety. The guardrail focuses on a narrow set of high-leverage issues that can cause big problems such as inverted error checks, ignored failures, and resource mishandling or suspicious contributor activity. The example also shows the value of layered safeguards. We don’t just accept an upstream version and hope for the best. The AI guardrail scrutinizes what changed. Our policy treats high-confidence findings as a hard stop. Humans then verify, reproduce the behavior, and apply the smallest correct patch. Only then does the release move forward. The best security is proactive, not reactive. As I alluded to above, there’s an important broader open source benefit to our AI guardrails. DHI depends on hundreds of community projects, some of which are downloaded millions or even billions of times per month. When the DHI AI guardrail surfaces an issue, our default is to fix it upstream rather than carry a private patch. That keeps our images clean, reduces long-term maintenance, and gives every downstream user a better baseline. It also helps the upstream projects, which benefit from our AI anomaly detection, our human judgment, and our subsequent code fixes.So back to the title. DHI is crafted by engineers, but it is protected by AI. Our guardrail is an active and continuously improving AI that reads upstream diffs with context, recognizes risky patterns, scores confidence, and blocks merges that don’t pass muster. Well-applied AI helps the human work better with faster reviews, tighter patches, and fewer regressions.This is the partnership we’re optimizing for. Humans set intent, exercise design judgment, and ship features. AI enforces discipline at critical gates. Best of all, every catch feeds the model signals for future scans, so protections improve as our ecosystem evolves. Collectively, this adds to the security of the entire open source ecosystem. It’s a win for Docker, for our customers, and the community.

item.title: How to add MCP Servers to Gemini CLI with Docker MCP Toolkit
item.author: Ajeet Singh Raina
item.content:encoded:
In the rapidly evolving landscape of AI-assisted development, most developers continue to struggle with clunky web interfaces, resource-intensive IDEs, and fragmented toolchains. But what if we told you there’s a combination that pairs Google’s (in just 5 months) with Docker’s innovative MCP Toolkit, quietly revolutionizing how modern AI developers work?Enter the powerhouse duo: . This isn’t just another tool combination — it’s a paradigm shift that delivers developer AI assistance with zero complexity overhead. A one-time setup of just 5 minutes can save you 20 minutes per test scenario — that’s a 97% time reduction that compounds with every test you run.Let’s look at a concrete example: browser testing and performance analysis that developers do regularly can be simplified and completely automated. What used to require opening browsers, clicking through flows, analyzing DevTools, taking screenshots, and manually documenting buys can now happen in a single 30-second conversation.In this guide, you’ll learn how to:Set up Gemini CLI and connect it to Docker MCP ToolkitAutomate browser testing that discovers real bugs and creates documented GitHub issuesSee how Gemini CLI can analyze performance, capture screenshots, and report findings—all without leaving your terminalWith 220+ pre-built MCP servers, browser automation becomes as simple as having a conversation. No Selenium WebDriver configuration, no CI/CD pipeline complexity, no manual screenshot management — just natural language instructions that execute real browser tests.Why Gemini CLI and Docker MCP Toolkit Work Better TogetherManual browser testing and performance analysis are broken. You open Chrome DevTools, click through pages, check network requests, analyze performance metrics, take screenshots, write bug reports, and then manually create GitHub issues. This context-switching nightmare wastes hours every sprint.Traditional automation tools don’t solve the real problem. Selenium requires brittle selectors and a complex setup. Playwright needs JavaScript knowledge and test frameworks. Both require maintaining test scripts that break with every UI change. The “solution” often takes more time than manual testing.While Gemini provides powerful AI capabilities and MCP provides the protocol, Docker MCP Toolkit makes browser automation practical. Without containerization, setting up browser testing means managing Chrome/Firefox installations, dealing with WebDriver versions, configuring Node.js dependencies, handling screenshot directories manually, and different configurations for every developer’s machine. The setup that should take 2 minutes takes 2-6 hours per developer.Docker MCP Toolkit eliminates this friction:220+ pre-built MCP servers in the catalog through Docker Desktop with browsers pre-installed (Chrome, Firefox, WebKit) for automated issue creation for artifact storageSecure credential management via OAuth or encrypted storage across Mac, Windows, and Linux when new server versions are releasedWe built Docker MCP Toolkit to meet developers where they are. If you’re using Gemini CLI, you should be able to automate browser testing without wrestling with infrastructure.Your tests run securely on your machine. Everything executes in isolated Docker containers on your local system. Test data, screenshots, and application access never leave your computer. No cloud uploads, no third-party services, no compliance concerns. You get enterprise-grade browser automation with complete privacy.Setting up Gemini CLI in Docker MCP ToolkitStep 1. Install Gemini CLInpm install -g @google/gemini-cli Step 2. Launch and authenticateOnce installed, just type gemini in your terminal window.Step 3. Log in via GoogleSelect a preferred theme style from the options.Choose a login method. I recommend “Login with Google”, which allows up to 60 requests/minute and 1,000 requests/day for freeIn case you need higher rate limits or enterprise access, I suggested you use an API key from Google AI Studio. You can easily set it as an environment variable:export GEMINI_API_KEY="YOUR_API_KEY" After selecting your sign-in method, a browser window will open. Simply log in with your Google accountStep 4. Start chatting with GeminiJust type “gemini” in your terminal window to start chatting with Gemini and enter your prompt.Connect Gemini CLI to Docker MCP ToolkitOption 1: One-Click Connection (Recommended)Navigate to in the sidebarFind “Gemini” in the list.Docker Desktop automatically configures the MCP Gateway connection – the underlying infrastructure that routes requests between Gemini CLI and your MCP servers, handling authentication, containerisation, and secure communication seamlessly.Option 2: Manual Command Line SetupIf you prefer a command-line setup or need to configure a specific project:Navigate to your project folder in the terminaldocker mcp client connect gemini --global You’ll see output like this:=== System-wide MCP Configurations === ● gemini: connected MCP_DOCKER: Docker MCP Catalog (gateway server) (stdio) ● gordon: connected MCP_DOCKER: Docker MCP Catalog (gateway server) (stdio)
You might have to restart 'gemini'. The status confirms Gemini CLI is linked to the Docker MCP Gateway.What’s happening under the hood?The Gemini CLI uses the mcpServers configuration in your settings.json file to locate and connect to MCP servers. This configuration supports multiple servers with different transport mechanisms. The mcpServers object is where you define each MCP server you want the CLI to connect to.Whenever you hit the “Connect” button under the Docker MCP Client for Gemini CLI, it adds the following Docker MCP Gateway configuration to the file.{ "theme": "Default", "selectedAuthType": "oauth-personal", "mcpServers": { "MCP_DOCKER": { "command": "docker", "args": ["mcp", "gateway", "run"], "env": {} } } } Learn more about MCP and Gemini CLI interaction through this link.Step 5. Restart Gemini CLI# Exit Gemini CLI if running, then restart gemini Step 6. Verify the ConnectionInside Claude Code, type to see available MCP servers. You should see the Docker MCP Gateway listed, which provides access to all enabled MCP servers. The tools indicate a successful connection. As you enable more MCP servers in Docker Desktop, they’ll appear here automatically.First Run: What to ExpectWhen you start Gemini CLI for the first time after connecting to Docker MCP Toolkit, you’ll see a prompt about the new MCP server: (recommended). This configures your project to automatically use Docker MCP Toolkit and any MCP servers you enable in Docker Desktop. You won’t need to approve MCP servers individually each time.You’re now ready to use Gemini with MCP servers from Docker Desktop.Real-World Demo: Automated Browser Testing and Performance AnalysisNow that you’ve connected Gemini CLI to Docker MCP Toolkit, let’s see it in action with a practical example. We’ll automatically discover real bugs through browser testing and identify performance bottlenecks through detailed analysis — the kind that would take 20 minutes of manual testing, DevTools monitoring, and performance profiling.What Makes This Realistic?This isn’t a trivial “Hello World” demo. We’re performing comprehensive browser testing and performance analysis on a real e-commerce application with the kinds of issues you encounter in production:Uses actual application running on localhostExecutes functional browser tests (navigation, element inspection, console monitoring)Discovers genuine performance bottlenecks through browser DevTools analysisIdentifies accessibility violations that affect real usersCaptures evidence with screenshots and console logsMeasures real performance metrics: page load times, network requests, resource usageCreates properly formatted GitHub issues with actionable recommendationsManual process: ~20 minutes (opening browsers, clicking through flows, DevTools analysis, performance profiling, documentation, issue creation)Automated with Gemini CLI + MCP: ~30 seconds totalThat’s a , but more importantly, it’s consistent, thorough, and documented every time.The application is a realistic e-commerce catalog with intentional issues that mirror common production problems:No pagination – loads all 15 products at once (will degrade with scale)Duplicate API calls – requests twice unnecessarilyMissing optimization – unoptimized loading patternsMissing product images – placeholder buttons instead of actual imagesVague button labels – “Fetch” and “Upload” aren’t descriptive for screen readersMissing ARIA labels – table structure not properly announcedMissing favicon – generates 404 errors in consoleConsole warnings – duplicate request warningsLet’s see if Gemini CLI can discover all of these automatically through intelligent browser testing and performance analysis, then create a comprehensive GitHub issue.Step 1: Set Up a Real-World e-Commerce Catalog applicationFor this demo, we’ll use a real e-commerce catalog application. This gives us realistic performance and accessibility issues to discover.git clone https://github.com/ajeetraina/catalog-service-node cd catalog-service-node # Start Docker services (database, S3, Kafka) docker compose up -d
# Install dependencies npm install --omit=optional
# Start the application npm run dev To make testing realistic, create sample products:# Create seed script cat > seed-data.sh << 'EOF' #!/bin/bash API_URL="http://localhost:3000/api"
echo "Seeding test products..."
curl -s -X POST "$API_URL/products" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"name":"Vintage Camera","description":"Classic 35mm film camera","price":299.99,"upc":"CAM001"}' \ > /dev/null && echo "✅ Vintage Camera"
curl -s -X POST "$API_URL/products" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"name":"Rare Vinyl Record - LAST ONE!","description":"Limited edition. Only 1 left!","price":149.99,"upc":"VINYL001"}' \ > /dev/null && echo "✅ Rare Vinyl Record"
curl -s -X POST "$API_URL/products" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"name":"Professional DSLR Camera","description":"50MP camera with 8K video","price":2499.99,"upc":"CAMPRO001"}' \ > /dev/null && echo "✅ Professional DSLR"
# Add bulk test products for i in {4..15}; do curl -s -X POST "$API_URL/products" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d "{\"name\":\"Test Product $i\",\"description\":\"Bulk test product $i\",\"price\":$((50 + RANDOM % 450)).99,\"upc\":\"BULK$(printf '%03d' $i)\"}" \ > /dev/null && echo "✅ Test Product $i" done
echo "" TOTAL=$(curl -s "$API_URL/products" | jq '. | length') echo "Total products: $TOTAL" echo "Ready! Visit http://localhost:5173" EOF
chmod +x seed-data.sh ./seed-data.sh
Seeding test products... ✅ Vintage Camera ✅ Rare Vinyl Record ✅ Professional DSLR ✅ Test Product 4 ✅ Test Product 5 ... ✅ Test Product 15
Total products: 15 Ready! Visit http://localhost:5173
Now you have a realistic environment with 15 products to analyze.For browser testing and performance analysis automation, you’ll orchestrate three MCP servers:Let’s configure each one.Configure Playwright MCP (Browser Automation)The Playwright MCP server gives Gemini the ability to control real browsers, Chrome, Firefox, and WebKit, just like a human would.Open → → Search for or Find Playwright (Browser Automation) in the resultsThe server will be added with default configuration (no additional setup needed)21+ browser automation tools including: – Navigate to URLs – Capture page state for analysis – Save visual evidencebrowser_click, browser_type – Interact with elements – Get console errors – Analyze HTTP requestsThe Playwright MCP runs in a secure Docker container with browsers pre-installed. No manual ChromeDriver setup, no WebDriver conflicts, no OS-specific browser installations.Configure GitHub MCP (Issue Creation)The GitHub MCP enables Gemini to create issues, PRs, and manage repositories on your behalf.Option 1: OAuth Authentication (Recommended – Easiest)In → , search Go to the tab in Docker DesktopYour browser opens GitHub’s authorization pageClick on GitHubYou’re redirected back to Docker DesktopReturn to tab, find No manual token creation. Authorization happens through GitHub’s secure OAuth flow with automatic token refresh.Option 2: Personal Access Token (For Granular Control)If you prefer manual control or need specific scopes:Step 1: Create GitHub Personal Access TokenClick your → Scroll to in the left sidebarClick → Click → “Generate new token (classic)”Name it: “Docker MCP Browser Testing” (Full control of repositories) (Update GitHub Actions workflows)Copy the token immediately (you won’t see it again!)Step 2: Configure in Docker DesktopIn → , find Click (if not already added)Select as the authentication methoddocker mcp secret set GITHUB.PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN=github_pat_YOUR_TOKEN_HERE Configure Filesystem MCP (Screenshot Storage)The Filesystem MCP allows Gemini to save screenshots and test artifacts to your local machine.Find and click Go to the tabUnder , add your project directory: Example: /Users/yourname/catalog-service-nodeOr wherever you cloned the repositoryYou can add multiple paths by clicking the Only grant access to directories you’re comfortable with Gemini reading and writing to. The Filesystem MCP is scoped to these specific paths for your protection.Change directory to the root of the project repository and open Gemini CLI:Navigate to http://host.docker.internal:5173 and perform a detailed performance and accessibility analysis:
1. Take a screenshot of the full page 2. Count how many products are displayed 3. Open browser DevTools and check: - Console for any JavaScript errors - Network tab: how many HTTP requests are made? - Performance: how long does the page take to load? 4. Identify performance issues: - Are all products loading at once with no pagination? - Are images optimized? - Any unnecessary network requests? 5. Check for accessibility issues: - Missing alt text on images - Color contrast problems - Vague button labels
Create a GitHub issue titled "Product catalog performance and accessibility issues" with: - Screenshots attached - List of specific problems found - Severity: Medium - Labels: performance, accessibility, enhancement Here’s what happens behind the scenes as Gemini orchestrates multiple MCP servers:Step 1: Browser NavigationThe web server is running. I'll navigate to http://host.docker.internal:5173, take a screenshot, and then proceed with the rest of the performance analysis. Gemini launches a real Chromium browser in the Docker container and navigates to your application.✦ I can see 15 products listed on the page: * Vintage Camera * Rare Vinyl Record - LAST ONE! * Professional DSLR Camera * Test Product 4-15 Step 3: Performance Check✦ Checking browser console and network requests... Opening DevTools to analyze performance metrics... ✦ Creating GitHub issue with findings...
create_issue (MCP_DOCKER MCP Server) {"labels":["performance","accessibility","enhancement"],"body":"### Summary..."}
✦ I have successfully created the issue. You can find it at: https://github.com/ajeetraina/catalog-service-node/issues/1 The Complete GitHub IssueGemini automatically created this comprehensive issue with structured information:Workflow Comparison: Before vs After MCPPaste prompt in Gemini CLIGemini analyzes automaticallyWrite detailed bug reportTime saved per test: 19.5 minutes (97% faster!)Per day (5 tests): 97 minutes saved → 1.6 hoursPer week (25 tests): 8 hours saved → 1 full workdayPer sprint (50 tests): 16 hours saved → 2 full workdaysPer year (1,000 tests): 325 hours saved → 40 workdaysYou’ve just witnessed how Docker MCP Toolkit transforms Gemini CLI from a chat assistant into a complete browser testing and performance analysis platform. What used to require opening browsers, clicking through flows, analyzing DevTools, documenting bugs, and creating issues manually now happens in one 30-second conversation.The combination of Gemini CLI and Docker MCP Toolkit represents a paradigm shift in AI-assisted development. By leveraging terminal-native tools and containerized services, you get: in tool selection with minimal overheadFuture-proof architecture that scales with your needsThis setup isn’t just about convenience — it’s about building a development environment that adapts to your workflow rather than forcing you to adapt to it. The developer productivity revolution is here. The question isn’t whether you’ll adopt AI-assisted development — it’s whether you’ll lead with the best tools available or play catch-up later.Ready to try it? Enable Docker MCP Toolkit in Docker Desktop and start building your own Gemini-powered development workflow today.

item.title: Private Kubernetes Access with Tailscale
item.author: Tony Chan
item.content:encoded: Kubernetes has been one of the hottest tools on the market, which a DevOps Engineer works on daily. It is powerful and convenient on the good side, but it means risks on the bad side. Access control to Kubernetes clusters has been a challenging and unstoppable topic nowadays. Doing it incorrectly is basically handing it out to the bad guys.There have been solutions like VPNs, SSH tunnels, or complex network rules. They all work, but could be fragile, hard to maintain, or lack scalability. We need a simple and zero-trust way to reach our clusters privately.So, I think of Tailscale… it could be the perfect solution! In this article, I will go through the setup with you so that you can access your Kubernetes cluster privately using Tailscale.According to their website,Tailscale is a mesh VPN (Virtual Private Network) service that streamlines connecting devices and services securely across different networks. It enables encrypted point-to-point connections using the open source WireGuard protocol, which means only devices on your private network can communicate with each other.Easier to understand, TailscaleBuilt on WireGuard, identity-basedNo need to expose ports or open inbound firewallCompared to traditional ways to privatise your clusters, they offer a simpler way that creates a private mesh network between devices and servers, so that your cluster’s API are reachable from anywhere.You may say, the traffic within this mesh network is still going through the Internet. You are not wrong. The traffic or the packets are end-to-end encrypted with the private key only stores in the servers or devices. You can say it’s not physically isolated, but it’s still a private network in a different way.To set up the private access, Tailscale Operator runs inside the Kubernetes cluster as a Pod. It connects the cluster’s internal network to your Tailnet, which is a private space belongs to your interconnected users, devices, and resources. Once connected, your machine can access the Kubernetes API as if it were on the same LAN.The architecture can be broken down into four components:Your device running Tailscale, like a laptopTailscale network, or Tailnet, that connected all authenticated nodesKubernetes cluster nodes, that runs Tailscale Operator to join the TailnetKubernetes API server, that remains internalHere is the simple flow to showcase the process,You authenticate your local device with TailscaleThe Kubernetes nodes runs authenticated Tailscale operator as a PodBoth appear as peers on the same Tailnet networkUsing kubectl to connect to Kubernetes API serverAs a zero-trust solution, no public IPs or open ports required. The access control is totally based on device identity and ACLs which configures through the Tailscale console. And most important is, all the communications are authenticated and encrypted.Before you start the configuration, please make sure the following prerequisites are fulfilled.A running Kubernetes clusterLocal machine with Tailscale CLI, kubectl, and Helm installedTailscale account with HTTPS enabled in your TailnetTailscale Setup and Cluster AccessTo connect the Kubernetes cluster to your Tailnet, we will need to use the Tailscale Operator. What it capable is not limited to enable the private cluster access, you can check their page for the full capabilities.Before we deploy the operator on the cluster, we need to pre-configure the access control and create OAuth client credentials so that the operator can authenticate to the Tailnet.On the Tailscale console, you should see a Access controls tab, and a option to use JSON editor.Make sure you add the tags here, and allow them to connect. The following is configs have been tested working for demo purposes."tagOwners": { "tag:k8s-operator": [], "tag:k8s": ["tag:k8s-operator"], }, // Allow all connections for demo purposes // You can restrict it with specific settings here { "dst": ["*"], }Then, go to the settings page, and choose OAuth clients on the side menu to create a new OAuth client with Devices Core and Auth Keys write scopes, and the tag you add in the access controls page.Don’t forget to keep the clientId and clientSecret, we will need them in the deployment.Now we are ready to deploy the Operator. Tailscale provides a Helm Chart for the deployment.First, add the Helm repository.helm repo add tailscale https://pkgs.tailscale.com/helmchartshelm repo updateThen, install the Helm Chart.helm upgrade \ --install \ tailscale/tailscale-operator \ --create-namespace \ --set-string oauth.clientId="<OAauth client ID>" \ --set-string oauth.clientSecret="<OAuth client secret>" \ --set-string apiServerProxyConfig.mode="true" \ --waitFinally, verify if the pods are running.kubectl get pods -n tailscaleYou should also see the operator has been connected to the Tailnet.Right now your cluster has been connected to the Tailnet through the operator. Before we can use the API server, we need a clusterrolebinding that binds to your Tailscale user.#You should adust the cluster role based on your needs.#I use `cluster-admin` for demo purposes.kubectl create clusterrolebinding <Your Preferred Binding Name> \  ✔ │ took 19m 19s │ at tailscale-operator.tailb0b12.ts.net ⎈ │ at 00:39:38  --clusterrole=cluster-admin \ --user="<Tailscale Username>"Run the Tailscale CLI to configure kubectl for you.tailscale configure kubeconfig tailscale-operatorThere will be a new context in your local Kube config. Check the current context and test it out!kubectl config get-contextskubectl config current-contextYou’ve just built a private, identity-based connection to your Kubernetes cluster without opening a single public port. Using Tailscale, your kubectl commands now securely tunnel through a WireGuard-based mesh network that’s only accessible to authenticated devices.This approach removes the need for static VPNs, jump hosts, or exposed API endpoints. Every connection is encrypted end-to-end, tied to real user identities, and auditable through Tailscale’s admin console. It’s simple to set up, easy to maintain, and scales from a single developer to an entire team.From here, you can take it further like integrating ACLs to manage fine-grained access, using Tailscale SSH for node management, or deploying the setup across multiple clusters. You can even automate everything with Terraform or GitOps workflows.

item.title: GitOps on Azure: Deploying AKS and ArgoCD with Automated SSL via Terraform [Part 1]
item.author: Muhammad Ateeb Aslam
item.content:encoded: Most teams set up ArgoCD manually, then later think about adding SSL. But what if you could bring up your entire GitOps setup such as AKS, ArgoCD, and SSL, with a single Terraform apply?In this post I’ll show the exact approach I used: provision AKS with Terraform, deploy ArgoCD via Helm (also from Terraform), create DNS records automatically, and wire up cert-manager + a ClusterIssuer for automated TLS issuance from Let’s Encrypt.I’ll also explain how Ingress, IngressClass, Ingress Controller and cert-manager interact so you understand the , not just the .Prerequisites / What you NeedYou should be familiar with Terraform basics.Tools & versions I used: Terraform >= 1.1.5, azurerm provider (v4.40.0), Helm provider, Kubernetes provider.You’ll need Azure subscription permissions to create resource groups, AKS, DNS zones and public IPs.1. Provisioning AKS with TerraformI put each major concern into its own module: AKS, ArgoCD, and ArgoCD-DNS. That keeps responsibilities clear and makes reuse easier.📦 AKS-Project/├── main.tf├── variables.tf├── config/│ ├── AKS/ │ ├── variables.tf │ │ ├── argocd-values.yaml │ ├── variables.tf │ ├── main.tf └── outputs.tfWe first need an AKS cluster, so let’s see what will go inside the of Key resource: azurerm_kubernetes_cluster. I’ll show the trimmed, relevant bits and some important settings I used for a small learning cluster(note: these are production defaults).resource "azurerm_kubernetes_cluster" "aks_cluster" { location = var.location name = var.name dns_prefix = var.dns_name azure_policy_enabled = false custom_ca_trust_certificates_base64 = [] http_application_routing_enabled = false node_os_upgrade_channel = "NodeImage" oidc_issuer_enabled = false open_service_mesh_enabled = false private_cluster_enabled = false private_cluster_public_fqdn_enabled = false resource_group_name = var.resource_group_name role_based_access_control_enabled = true run_command_enabled = true sku_tier = "Free" auto_scaling_enabled = false host_encryption_enabled = false max_pods = 250 node_count = 2 node_public_ip_enabled = false only_critical_addons_enabled = false orchestrator_version = "1.32" os_disk_size_gb = 150 os_disk_type = "Ephemeral" os_sku = "Ubuntu" scale_down_mode = "Delete" type = "VirtualMachineScaleSets" ultra_ssd_enabled = false vm_size = "Standard_D4d_v4" drain_timeout_in_minutes = 0 max_surge = "10%" node_soak_duration_in_minutes = 0 } identity_ids = [] type = "SystemAssigned" } key_vault_secrets_provider { secret_rotation_enabled = true network_plugin = "azure" network_plugin_mode = "overlay" } default_nginx_controller = "AnnotationControlled" dns_zone_ids = [] }Notice that we are creating this AKS cluster for learning purposes only and that’s why we have:node_count of 2 nodes which is reasonable for a small cluster.vm_size is Standard_D4d_v4scale_down_mode set to "Delete" which means we are telling Azure to delete the node when scaling down (Not recommended in Production)web_app_routing is helpful if we want Azure-managed app routing. If using a custom Ingress controller (NGINX, AGIC), we need to configure accordingly.Our AKS configuration for terraform is ready and the relevant etc. files can be found in my Github repo shared above.we can now do a “” now and our AKS cluster will be created inside Azure.Once the Terraform apply completes, we have a fully functional AKS cluster. Now comes the GitOps layer.2. Deploying ArgoCD via Helm using TerraformLet’s recall our directory structure explained above. We have just deployed only one module which is “AKS”. Now we need to deploy ArgoCD on top of AKS. so let’s start writing some code for the ArgoCD module.In order to deploy ArgoCD in AKS, we will do the following:Create a namespace named “” using resourceDeploy ArgoCD helm chart using resource.So, in we will first define the required providers for this module:terraform { required_version = ">= 1.1.5" kubernetes = { source = "hashicorp/kubernetes" version = "~> 2.32" helm = { source = "hashicorp/helm" version = "~> 3.0.2" kubectl = { source = "gavinbunney/kubectl" version = "~> 1.14" }Notice that I have also included the and providers.2.1: Creating ArgoCD NamespaceLet’s first create a in which our ArgoCD related stuff will be created. To create namespace, we will use the resource from kubernetes provider.resource "kubernetes_namespace" "argocd" { metadata { name = var.namespace #argocd labels = { "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "argocd" }}name will be argocd which i defined in the file present at root (I have already shown this file in above section).2.2: Deploying ArgoCD using helm chartLet’s now deploy the ArgoCD helm chart inside “argocd” namespace we created earlier.resource "helm_release" "argocd_deploy" { name = "argocd" repository = "https://argoproj.github.io/argo-helm" chart = "argo-cd" version = "7.6.8" # Use latest stable version namespace = kubernetes_namespace.argocd.metadata[0].name # Wait for deployment to complete wait = true timeout = 600 templatefile("${path.module}/argocd-values.yaml", { hostname = var.argocd_config.hostname ingress_class_name = var.argocd_config.ingress_class_name redis_ha_enabled = var.argocd_config.redis_ha_enabled autoscaling_enabled = var.argocd_config.autoscaling_enabled notifications_enabled = var.argocd_config.argocd_notifications_enabled cluster_issuer = var.argocd_config.cluster_issuer ] depends_on = [kubernetes_namespace.argocd]}Look at the block, this is where we define values required by the argocd helm chart. and we are defining these values inside our file. Below is my argocd-values.yaml:global: domain: ${hostname} replicas: 1 %{ if autoscaling_enabled } resources: cpu: 500m requests: memory: 256Mi enabled: true replicas: 1 %{ if autoscaling_enabled } autoscaling: minReplicas: 1 targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 70 targetMemoryUtilizationPercentage: 70 %{ endif } type: ClusterIP enabled: ${hostname != "" ? "true" : "false"} ingressClassName: ${ingress_class_name} annotations: nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true" nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/backend-protocol: "HTTPS" cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: ${cluster_issuer} tls: true server.insecure: true application.instanceLabelKey: argocd.argoproj.io/instance replicas: 1 %{ if autoscaling_enabled } autoscaling: minReplicas: 1 targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 50 targetMemoryUtilizationPercentage: 50 %{ endif } enabled: true enabled: true enabled: ${redis_ha_enabled} haproxy: replicas: 2 masterGroupName: argocd save: '""' sentinel: %{ endif }configs: policy.default: role:readonly p, role:admin, applications, *, */*, allow p, role:admin, clusters, *, *, allow p, role:admin, repositories, *, *, allow g, argocd-admins, role:adminIn order to maintain consistency, I am using also using variables in this file. See ${hostname}, ${ingress_class_name}, ${autoscaling_enabled}, ${redis_ha_enabled}, ${argocd_notifications_enabled} and ${cluster_issuer}.This small module creates an azurerm_dns_a_record that points your DNS zone to the public IP of the LoadBalancer created by the Ingress/Service. Without this automation you’d have to manually create the A record in the Azure Portal.Till Now, we are good with ArgoCD Setup. If we just do a “terraform apply”, we will have argocd deployed on our cluster. But since we need ingress and SSL also, there is a bit of work still left. Let’s focus on that.With this, ArgoCD is accessible inside the cluster, but we still need a public endpoint secured by SSL.4. Understanding the Confusing StuffIngress, IngressClass, IngressController, Cert-Manager, ClusterIssuer, TLS secret, Challenge, Order … Wait, What?I understand this confusion (and frustration that comes along!), but let’s unpack all these things step by step and understand them.We will go through the following bunch of Kubernetes objects in context of our project and see how they help the user’s web request to navigate through the cluster:This will help us understand what happens under the hood, before the user is able to access this beautiful UI of ArgoCD:4.1: Creation of Ingress Resource:Now, I want you to closely look at this section in our file:ingress: enabled: ${hostname != "" ? "true" : "false"} ingressClassName: ${ingress_class_name} annotations: nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true" nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/backend-protocol: "HTTPS" cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: ${cluster_issuer} tls: trueHere, we’re enabling the Ingress configuration for ArgoCD. When ingress.enabled is set to true, the Helm chart automatically creates an along with the other ArgoCD components (like the server, repo, and application controller).you could simply hardcode "true" for enabled.But notice the condition I added:enabled: ${hostname != "" ? "true" : "false"}“Only enable the Ingress if a hostname has been provided.”If ${hostname} is empty, Ingress creation will be skipped entirely thus preventing invalid or half-configured resources.Once this configuration is applied, an similar to the one below will be created inside “argocd” namespace:apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1kind: Ingress name: argocd-server labels: app.kubernetes.io/name: argocd-server app.kubernetes.io/part-of: argocd nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true" nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/backend-protocol: "HTTPS" cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: letsencrypt-prod ingressClassName: webapprouting.kubernetes.azure.com - hosts: secretName: argocd-server-tls - host: testdomain.xyz.com paths: pathType: Prefix service: port:4.2: Ingress vs IngressController ?We have just finished creating an or but In order to route traffic from outside the cluster to a specific pod running inside the cluster, we need something that can do the routing. Normally we handle routing by using proxy servers (e.g NGINX). Same concept applies here. We need some proxy server that can do the actual routing.The Ingress resource doesn’t directly handle any traffic itself.Instead, it’s like a for the Ingress Controller.Ingress Controller is the actual thing that does the heavy lifting i-e routing traffic. In Kubernetes, computation and application logic only happen inside pods, that’s where containers run your code. Everything outside pods — Ingress resources, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets, CRDs, etc.are configurations. They define traffic flows, settings to apply, and rules pods should enforce. For example, an Ingress resource doesn’t itself route traffic , it simply describes routing rules. The Ingress Controller (which runs as pods) watches these Ingress resources and dynamically configures itself.The IngressController is here what does the work of PROXY server. IngressController is just a set of Pods doing the routing job.An isn’t a special Kubernetes object, it’s just a set of pods (and usually a LoadBalancer service) running a proxy like NGINX or Traefik. Its job is to watch the Kubernetes API for Ingress resources and dynamically configure routing rules inside the proxy.Kubernetes doesn’t inherently know how to route HTTP traffic, the controller provides that logic.That’s why you can’t do kubectl get ingresscontrollers; it’s not a resource type, it’s a running component/Pod.We now know what and is, but to make concepts more clear, I have made this Diagram that can help you know exactly how the routing takes place and how the components like ingress, ingressController and fits in.When we used the add-on while creating AKS cluster, it automatically provisions the IngressController part for us i-e The box with a set of Nginx Pods. Once the IngressController is created, it starts advertising itself inside cluster using an IngressClass name. So as soon as the Controller pods came live, they are like:“Hey, I want to be referenced by the so if you want to configure me, make sure to reference this ingressclass in ingress resource”See the below Kubernetes Config and notice how the Ingress Controller pod advertise itself using an ingress class:Next, we create Ingress Resource () by using helm-chart of ArgoCD. The ingress resource shall also refer the same so that the right pods shall be updated with the configuration defined by Ingress Resource. See the kubernetes config of Ingress:The IngressClass acts like a “bridge” between the Ingress resource and the Ingress Controller. It defines should handle a particular Ingress. This becomes important when you have more than one controller in your cluster, for example, one NGINX ingress for public apps and another for internal traffic. The ingressClassName field in the Ingress manifest tells Kubernetes which controller to hand off the routing to.Now only one missing piece left. Who will send traffic to the IngressController so that IngressController can route the traffic to right pods using its configured routing rules? OR What’s the entry Point into the cluster?It’s LoadBalancer Service. The Loadbalancer service uses the “selector” label to “send” traffic to IngressController Pods.This LoadBalancer IP gets a public IP from Cloud Provider (Azure), which we use as A record for the domain we decided to use to ArgoCD.Who Creates this LoadBalancer Service ? The answer is, AKS’s webapprouting addon automatically creates a loadbalancer service along with ingress Controller pods.Till Now, According to our Flow, we will be able to access ArgoCD UI using the domain, but still the SSL is missing and for that we need to understand Cert-Manager and Cluster-Issuer.I believe this is a good place to pause, the setup so far gives us a fully running ArgoCD on AKS.In the next part, we’ll dive into and how they work under the hood and how they complete the end-to-end routing and SSL flow.Thanks for reading. If this sparked even a tiny thought in you, imagine what the next one might do. So clap, follow, and subscribe, and let’s keep building ideas together.

item.title: How to deploy a Dash app through docker onto Heroku?
item.author: Guangyuan(Frank) Li
item.content:encoded: Built a publicly accessible web appI recently had a task to deploy an academic web tool that I built to a publicly facing domain so that people can use it, I thought it should be an easy task but turns out deployment is extremely tricky and there aren’t many tutorials detailing the steps. So if you are facing the same issue, this blog might help.Although this tutorial is specifically written based on Python Dash app, through docker container and deploy to Heroku, I think it might be generalizable for other usages as well.Step 1: You need a working Dash app (Python code)This tutorial is not meant to replace the rich resource on how to build a dash app in python, you may refer to their official tutorial . But first and foremost, you need to have a working Dash app, which usually means a python script in which you can execute and the app will appear in your localhost page. In my case, this is my python script:Step 2: You need to dockerize your appAlthough Heroku platform accepts app hosted on github page as well, it will have 500MB storage limit and based on my experience the dependencies often cause issue, dockerize your app will solve all these problem which to me is a more stable solution. There are two necessary adjustments you need to make for your code which are extremely important to assure the smooth deployment.Using production server gunicornDon’t hardcode port but dynamically capture it on the flyuse dash.no_update or None for edge case event, error will not be tolerable in production serverFirst of all, the development server that dash has will not work, so you need to use production server gunicorn , which can be installed through pip and use gunicore to execute the app code. Asides from that, pay attention to following code block, make sure to add server=app.server line and follow how I write app.run() .The way port is defined is what I meant by dynamically capture the $PORT because heroku will automatically assign your app a port instead of fixed port, so os.environ.get('PORT') assures that automaically assigned port will be used in the app, 5000 is not important, it is just a fallback value.app = Dash(__name__,assets_folder='/assets')server = app.server # this is importantapp.title = 'ImmunoVerse'app.layout....if __name__ == '__main__': # this is important app.run(debug=False,host='0.0.0.0',port=int(os.environ.get('PORT',5000))) # this is importantNow you need to talk about the command to execute the app:gunicorn --bind 0.0.0.0:$PORT web:serverLastly as I was explaining, when you are debugging the dash code using development server, any error won’t break your code or let your app exit out, but it won’t be case in production server, so you need to preemptively code such edge case, for instance, when you change your input, the old value might be carried over in the new data table, an error might occur, in the case like that, particularly handle such situation using dash.no_update or NoneType instead of triggering an error.This tutorial is also not a repetition for how to write Dockerfile, the process is just the same as how you would dockerize any python app by starting with a pulling base image, installing core dependencies, python dependencies (including gunicorn), but remember the CMD line should be running gunicore if that makes sense:CMD /opt/conda/envs/python_env/bin/gunicorn --bind 0.0.0.0:$PORT web:serverAt the end of this step, you should have a compliant dash app python script and your Dockerfile ready to be run.There are many options out there when it comes to which server to use for web deployment, such as AWS, GCP and many others. The annoying part to me is each platform requires you to create their own accounts, setting up billing accounts and learn their own deployment tools. The learning curve is high and what you learned won’t be immediately transferrable when it comes to another platform. For me, I am not a professional web developer but just want to make my scientific finding/resources more accessible, and I found heroku platform works very well for me.Gave your app a name and when selecting deployment method, choose Heroku CLI not connect to Github, because we are using dockerized app.You can also toggle different tabs to view the metrics (request per second, cpu load, memory usage, etc) and the spec of the server, I normally go for Basic which is $7/month.2. Download the Heroku CLI for your laptop (Mac M chip for me), decompress the tarball and the executable heroku should be in the decompressed folder, particulary in bin folder, you can add this executable to your path, or just call the full path /path/to/heroku/bin/heroku in later steps3. Now we finally gonna start the deployment (immunoverse-web is my web app name, adjust accordingly)heroku loginheroku container:loginheroku stack:set container --app immunoverse-web # change to container only need to do once# becasue of apply M1 chip which is ARM architechturedocker buildx inspect --bootstrapdocker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 --no-cache -t registry.heroku.com/immunoverse-web/web --load .docker push registry.heroku.com/immunoverse-web/webheroku container:release web --app immunoverse-webheroku open --app immunoverse-webheroku logs --tail --app immunoverse-webNow you should have your app running and a web browser will pop up.Step 4: (Advanced) Increase booting timeBy default, Python app only has 60s for your old version to retire and set up the new release. If you found the booting time to be too short, you can increase it by visiting this page. For me, I increase to 90s just to be safe, and you can also consider pre-boot option as detailed here.Step 5: (Advanced) Register a paid URL domainThere are certain many different ways, I went to Squarespace and purchased a domain name.How to connect these two, the heroku app domain and the purchased public domain?heroku domains:add www.immuno-verse.com --app immunoverse-webheroku domains --app immunoverse-webStep 6: (Advanced) Adding SSL for HTTPS protocolBy default, your custom domain will only be accessible through http but not https protocol. To enable the latter, you need to first set up the SSL certificate for your heroku app:# enableheroku certs:auto:enable --app immunoverse-webheroku certs:auto --app immunoverse-web# SNI endpoint should now be setheroku domains --app immunoverse-webStep 7: (Advanced) Upgrade the dyno type# checkheroku ps --app immunoverse-webheroku dyno:resize web=standard-1x --app immunoverse-webThat marks the end of this tutorial, let me know if you have any questions!

item.title: We Wasted $200K on Kubernetes Before Switching to Heroku
item.author: Devrim Ozcay

item.title: Fixing the “ChunkLoadError” When Deploying Next.js on ECS: What Really Happens Behind the Scenes
item.author: Erind Hoxha
item.content:encoded: Have you ever deployed a Next.js app to ECS behind a load balancer, and right after deployment your QA tester reports:“The site just crashed after the deploy… but now it’s working again?”If your logs show 404 errors for missing chunk files, you’ve just met a subtle but nasty deployment bug that happens when Next.js, ECS, and load balancing meet each other.I recently ran into this at work, and after a day of debugging, testing, and Slack , we finally figured out what was really going on (and how to fix it properly).Our infrastructure setup looked like this:Next.js app deployed to Application Load Balancer (ALB) in front of multiple containersEach deployment spins up new containers before draining old onesStatic assets (/_next/static/...) are served from inside the containerRight after deployment, users started seeing this error:ChunkLoadError: Loading chunk 727 failed. (missing: https://staging.injuryfirmstl.com/_next/static/chunks/727-a38a538860ca6aba.js)Uncaught SyntaxError: Unexpected token '<'Refreshing the page didn’t help immediately, but after a few minutes… everything worked again. Magically.No new deploys. No code changes. Just time.We dove into the issue step by step:Chunks were missing only right after deployBut a few minutes later, they were accessible.No caching was configured on the load balancer., and even forcing Cache-Control: no-store didn’t help.The missing JS files appeared 5 to 10 seconds post-deploy, suggesting some kind of delay or race condition.After testing multiple times and checking logs in , we discovered the pattern:ECS spins up with the version of the app.The are still live for a short period (until drained).The sends some requests to old containers, and some to new ones.Next.js generates on every build, for example:The new build might rename that to:So if a user’s request for HTML lands on a , but their JS chunk request goes to an , that file doesn’t exist there anymore -> 404 ChunkLoadErrorbroken UI.Once the old containers are fully terminated, all traffic hits the new ones, and everything “magically” works again.Why Sticky Sessions Don’t Really Solve ItOur first idea was to enable on the Load Balancer.This ensures that all requests from a user go to the same container. It works , because:The user who hits the new container gets new chunks.The user who hits the old one stays with the old chunks.But it’s not bulletproof:Browsers open parallel connections that can hit different containers.Opening a new tab breaks the sticky session.Old containers might never drain if traffic keeps routing to them.So while it reduced the frequency of crashes, it didn’t them.The Real Fix: Move Static Assets to CloudFrontThe ultimate solution was to decouple static assets from containers entirely.After building the Next.js app in CI, we (.next/static, public assets, etc.) to an .We set up a in front of that bucket.We configured assetPrefix in next.config.js to point to the CloudFront URL.// next.config.jsconst nextConfig = { assetPrefix: "https://{{url.cloudfront.net}}",};Now, assets for both versions of the app live side by side, no matter which ECS container is serving the HTML.No more chunk 404s or hydration errors during deploysContainers serve only app code, not static filesCloudFront handles caching and delivery efficientlyBlue/green deployments become painlessIf your Next.js app deployed on ECS suddenly breaks right after deployment with chunk load errors, and then fixes itself, you’re likely hitting the mixed-version asset problem. quick fix, not bulletproof robust and future-proofServing static assets from a CDN like CloudFront completely solves the issue.This bug was sneaky because it only appeared briefly during deployment, making it look like a transient issue.But understanding how ECS load balancing works (and how Next.js builds its chunks) made the solution clear: separate the immutable assets from the deploy lifecycle.If you’ve hit this before, or have a better strategy for deploying Next.js apps on ECS, I’d love to hear your approach.

item.title: Docker Service Lifecycle Management and Network Optimisation: A Production-Grade Pseudocode…
item.author: Scaibu
item.content:encoded: Docker Service Lifecycle Management and Network Optimisation: A Production-Grade Pseudocode FrameworkA language-agnostic framework for managing and scaling containerised workloads reliably in production.This framework is designed around several core principles:Deterministic control loops — ensuring system state converges even after crashes.Explicit lifecycle management — create, start, stop, drain, upgrade, and remove containers safely.Network-aware placement and optimization — TCP tuning, MTU configuration, QoS enforcement, and congestion control.Failure management and resiliency — automatic restarts, replacement, and stateful failover.Observability-driven automation — metrics, alerts, autoscaling, and operator runbooks.Idempotency and transactional guarantees — safe retries, single-source-of-truth, and KV-backed locks.By leveraging these principles, operators can build control-plane components that are reliable, scalable, and extensible.The Service Registry is the authoritative source of truth for all containerised services. Its role is deceptively simple — track containers — but in reality, it faces many challenges: state drift, network consistency, failure recovery, observability, and concurrency. The registry can get out of sync with the Docker runtime if containers are removed manually or crash unexpectedly. Orchestration decisions, like scaling or failover, depend on accurate state. Stale information can lead to overloading, service downtime, or lost metrics. The Docker runtime is dynamic; multiple containers can start or stop simultaneously, making reconciliation non-trivial.Networking & Overlay Consistency Containers must be attached to the correct network overlays immediately upon start. Without proper networking, services cannot communicate, breaking the system. Network attachment must happen atomically with container registration while also handling failures during setup.Observability & Health Tracking Health probes, logging, and metrics must be initialized as containers are registered. Delayed observability can prevent detection of failing containers and cause cascading failures.Concurrency & Determinism Containers can be added, removed, or crash simultaneously. Updates must be deterministic; race conditions can cause duplicates, orphaned entries, or inconsistent states.Detect missing containers → remove from registry.Detect new containers → add to registry with metadata.Update container info → CPU, memory, network, health.Attach networking overlays.Attach health probes and metrics.Event-driven updates → low latency.Periodic sync → safety net for missed events.Handle failures during network attachment or probe setup.How do I know the registry is consistent?How do I define “consistent” in a dynamic container environment?Is “consistent” only about existence, or does it include health, network, and metrics?How fast should the registry respond to events?What is the tradeoff between latency and system load?How do we handle transient failures?Which failures should trigger retries vs alerts vs manual intervention?function syncRegistry(): while true: dockerContainers = listDockerContainers() registryContainers = getRegistryContainers() # Detect missing containers for container in registryContainers: if container.id not in dockerContainers: removeContainerFromRegistry(container) log("Removed container:", container.id) triggerRecoveryIfNeeded(container) for container in dockerContainers: if container.id not in registryContainers: containerInfo = createContainerInfo(container) if attachNetworkOverlay(containerInfo) is False: log("Network attach failed, retrying:", container.id) retryNetworkAttach(containerInfo) attachHealthProbes(containerInfo) addContainerToRegistry(containerInfo) log("Added container:", container.id) # Update existing containers for container in dockerContainers: if container.id in registryContainers: updateContainerInfo(container.id, container) ┌───────────────────────┐ │ Start Sync Loop │ │ ┌─────────────────────────┐ │ Fetch Docker State │ └─────────┬──────────────┘ ┌──────────────┴───────────────┐ ▼ ▼ Container in Registry? Not in Registry? / \ │ Yes No Add Container: │ │ - Create metadata ▼ ▼ - Attach networkUpdate Remove - Attach health probesMetadata from Registry - Add to registry ▼Sleep until next intervalfunction handleContainerEvent(event): containerId = event.containerId if containerId not in registry: containerInfo = createContainerInfo(event.container) success = attachNetworkOverlay(containerInfo) scheduleRetry(containerInfo, attachNetworkOverlay) attachHealthProbes(containerInfo) addContainerToRegistry(containerInfo) log("Container started:", containerId) else: updateContainerInfo(containerId, event.container) elif eventType in ["STOP", "CRASH"]: if containerId in registry: removeContainerFromRegistry(containerId) log("Container removed:", containerId) triggerRecoveryIfNeeded(containerId) elif eventType == "METRIC_UPDATE": updateMetrics(containerId, event.metrics) ┌─────────────────────┐ │ Container Event Recv│ │ ┌─────────────┐ └─────┬───────┘ ┌───────────┼───────────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ START STOP/CRASH METRIC_UPDATE │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼Add or Update Remove from Update MetricsContainer Registry Update HealthAttach Network │Check Scaling / RecoveryCritical Points & ChallengesAtomic Registration + NetworkingContainer unusable if registration succeeds but the network fails. Retry logic is critical.Health & Metrics Before OrchestrationMissing metrics may trigger incorrect scaling or recovery actions.Multiple simultaneous events require deterministic updates.Every sync/event must lead to the , or orchestration becomes unpredictable.Lifecycle Management ensures deterministic, reliable operations across the full lifespan of containers. This includes creating, starting, stopping, draining, restarting, removing, and updating containers in production-scale environments. The key challenge is managing complexity while maintaining consistency, availability, and safety.Node Allocation & Scheduling Choose the best node based on resource availability, affinity/anti-affinity, taints/tolerations, and load. Poor placement causes hotspots, degraded performance, or even failure to start containers. Nodes’ resources change dynamically; constraints must be computed in real time for multiple containers simultaneously.Balancing CPU, memory, GPU, and storage constraints.Co-locating containers to minimize latency while avoiding overloading.Deciding placement for update batches (rolling/canary).Image Pull & Retry Handling Container startup may fail if images cannot be pulled or layers are corrupted. Startup failures block deployments. Networks are unreliable, registries may throttle requests, and container images may be large.Implement exponential backoff with capped retries.Fallback to alternative registries if primary fails.Verify image integrity before container start. Stop containers without dropping in-flight requests. Abrupt stops cause downtime, failed requests, or inconsistent state. Requires coordination between load balancers, service discovery, and shutdown hooks.Mark container as “draining” before stop.Wait for active sessions to complete or timeout.Remove container from routing tables progressively. Handle failures automatically and deterministically. Must avoid cascading failures Multiple simultaneous failures require prioritization and scheduling of replacementsTrack restart count to avoid infinite loops.Apply cooldown periods between restartsUpdate registry state atomically after restart.Safe Updates (Rolling, Canary, Blue-Green) Deploy new container versions without downtime. Must maintain availability, health, and rollback capability. Requires monitoring, batch management, traffic routing, and rollback logic.Rolling updates: Update in small batches with health checks.Canary: Deploy a small subset and observe behavior before full rollout.Blue-green: Deploy parallel environment, switch traffic atomically, then remove old version.Automatically rollback if health or performance thresholds are breached.Node selection with bin-packing constraints and multi-resource awareness.Pull images with retries, exponential backoff, and integrity checks.Start container, attach network, initialize volumes, set environment variables.Drain container gracefully with load balancer coordination.Stop and remove container atomically, triggering replacement if required.Restart container with retry limits and cooldowns.Implement rolling, canary, and blue-green updates safely.Monitor container health during lifecycle operations to trigger rollback or scaling.Handle concurrent operations to avoid race conditions.Handle partial failures (network attach fails, image corrupt, or probe initialization fails)How to balance load while allocating nodes?When should a failed container be restarted vs replaced?How long should a container be allowed to drain before forced termination?How to detect failing canary containers quickly enough to prevent cascading issues?How to prioritize multiple lifecycle operations happening simultaneously?How to atomically switch traffic in blue-green deployments?Lifecycle Management Overview ┌─────────────────────┐ │ Start Lifecycle Op │ │ ┌─────────────────────┐ └─────┬───────────────┘ ┌───────┼───────┐ ┌───────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ CREATE STOP RESTART UPDATE │ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ Node Allocation Drain? Stop + Create Pull Image? (Bin-packing) │ │ Resource Check ▼ ▼ Select Node Stop Pull Image & Retry Check Affinity Remove Verify Integrity Check Load Schedule Start Container Reserve Resources Replacement ▼ ▼ Pull Image Monitor Health Retry & Backoff │ Start Container Restart if Needed Attach Health Probes ▼ Add to Registry Completed │ Monitor Health & Metrics ▼Check Scaling, Rollback, Recoveryfunction createContainer(service): node = selectNode(service.constraints) log("No suitable node available for service", service.name) return None imagePulled = pullImageWithRetries(service.image) if not imagePulled: log("Failed to pull image for service:", service.name) scheduleRetryPull(service) container = startContainer(service, node) attachNetworkOverlay(container) attachHealthProbes(container) initializeVolumes(container) configureEnvironment(container) addContainerToRegistry(container) log("Container started:", container.id, "on node:", node) return containerCreate & Start (Extended) ┌───────────────┐ │ Start CREATE │ │ Select Node (Constraints) Node Found? ── No ──> Retry Later ▼ │ ▼ ▼ Success Failed │ │ Start Container Schedule Retry ▼Attach Network & Health Probes │Initialize Volumes & Env ▼Add to Registry & Monitorfunction stopContainer(container, gracePeriod=30s): markContainerAsDraining(container) removeFromLoadBalancer(container) startDrainTimer(container, gracePeriod) while hasActiveRequests(container) and not timeout: wait(1s) # Wait for in-flight requests to complete forceStop(container) # Only after drain timeout removeFromRegistry(container) log("Container stopped:", container.id) ┌───────────────┐ │ Stop Container│ │ Mark container as draining ▼ Remove from load balancer │ Wait for in-flight requests or timeout Requests Timeout? ▼ ▼ Continue draining Force stop │ Remove from Registry ▼Rolling / Canary / Blue-Green Updatesfunction deployUpdate(service, newVersion, strategy): containers = service.getContainers() if strategy == "rolling": batches = batchContainers(containers, size=service.updateBatchSize) stopAndReplaceBatch(batch, newVersion) if failureDetected(batch): log("Rolling update failed, rolled back") elif strategy == "canary": canary = deployCanary(service, newVersion) if healthy(canary): deployRemaining(service, newVersion) else: log("Canary failed, update aborted") elif strategy == "blue-green": newEnv = deployNewEnvironment(service, newVersion) monitorHealth(newEnv) removeOldEnvironment(service) ┌───────────────┐ │ Deploy Update │ │ ▼ ▼ Strategy? Monitor Health / | \ Rolling Canary Blue-Green │ │ │ Batch Update Canary Deploy New Env │ │ │ Monitor Health Switch Traffic │ │ │ Failure? Healthy? Remove Old Env │ │ Rollback Deploy RemainingCritical Points & Challenges (Extended): Balancing constraints, affinity, load, and multi-resource requirements.: Must account for network issues, image corruption, and registry failures.: Must coordinate load balancers, service discovery, and shutdown hooks.Rolling/Canary/Blue-Green Updates: Require monitoring, rollback, batch management, and atomic traffic switching.Concurrency & Determinism: Multiple operations may occur simultaneously; race conditions must be avoided.: All operations must be resilient to partial failures without leaving inconsistent states.3. Placement and Node SelectionPlacement and Node Selection is the core of resource optimization in container orchestration. It decides which node a container should run on, directly impacting performance, resource utilization, network efficiency, and service reliability.While the task seems simple — pick a node with free CPU/memory — it is in reality in large-scale clusters due to dynamic workloads, multiple constraints, and latency-sensitive services.Resource Constraints & Bin-Packing Each node has finite CPU, memory, disk, and GPU resources. Containers have resource requirements that must fit. Overcommitting resources leads to throttling, degraded performance, or container failure.Nodes’ available resources change dynamically.Multiple containers may start at once, requiring simultaneous bin-packing decisions.Efficient packing must balance load across the cluster.Placement Constraints (Affinity / Anti-Affinity) Some containers must run together (affinity), or separately (anti-affinity), based on roles, services, or availability zones. Violating constraints can break service guarantees or reduce fault tolerance.Multiple constraints may conflict (e.g., anti-affinity vs resource availability).Must enforce constraints deterministically while still finding a feasible node.Network Locality & Latency Sensitivity Some services are latency-sensitive and should ideally be co-located in the same region or zone. Cross-region communication increases latency and reduces performance.Must score nodes based on proximity and network penalties.Needs to balance network efficiency with resource availability. Node resources, network bandwidth, and container placements are constantly changing. Node selection must adapt in real time.Requires fast scoring and filtering.Must avoid race conditions when multiple schedulers or lifecycle operations act simultaneously.Collect up-to-date resource metrics for CPU, memory, disk, GPU, and network.Score nodes based on available resources and constraints.Apply placement rules (affinity, anti-affinity, roles, regions).Apply network penalties for cross-region or high-latency nodes.Rank nodes deterministically to pick the “best fit.”Handle tie-breaking between equally suitable nodesDetect when no node satisfies all constraints and decide on retries or preemption.How to balance resource utilization with latency-sensitive placement?How to score nodes efficiently in real-time?How to resolve conflicts between multiple placement constraints?How to handle partial failures, e.g., a node suddenly becomes unavailable during placement?Should placement consider historical performance metrics or only current free resources?How to preempt lower-priority containers if no node fits a high-priority container?Placement & Node Selection Overview ┌─────────────────────┐ │ Start Node Selection│ │ ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ Filter Nodes by Constraints │ │ - CPU/Memory/GPU/Disk │ │ - Affinity / Anti-Affinity │ │ - Node Role / Region │ └─────────┬──────────────────┘ │ Nodes Available After Filter? Yes No ▼ ▼ Score Nodes Based on Retry / Queue / Alert - Free Resources - Latency Penalties ▼ Rank Nodes Deterministically │ Select Top Node ▼ Reserve Resources Atomically │ Return Nodefunction selectNode(container): candidateNodes = [] if not satisfiesResourceConstraints(node, container): if not satisfiesPlacementConstraints(node, container): score = calculateNodeScore(node, container) candidateNodes.append((node, score)) if candidateNodes is empty: log("No suitable node found for container", container.id) return None # Deterministically rank nodes by score candidateNodes.sort(by=scoreDescending) bestNode = candidateNodes[0].node if reserveResources(bestNode, container): return bestNode log("Failed to reserve resources on node", bestNode.id) return Nonefunction calculateNodeScore(node, container): cpuScore = node.freeCPU / node.totalCPU memoryScore = node.freeMemory / node.totalMemory networkPenalty = calculateNetworkPenalty(node, container) affinityBonus = checkAffinity(node, container) antiAffinityPenalty = checkAntiAffinity(node, container) totalScore = cpuScore*0.4 + memoryScore*0.3 + affinityBonus - antiAffinityPenalty - networkPenalty*0.3 return totalScore ┌───────────────┐ │ Calculate Node│ └─────┬─────────┘ ┌───────┼──────────┐ CPU Score Memory Score Network Penalty └───────┼──────────┘ Apply Affinity / Anti-Affinity ▼Critical Points & ChallengesMust prevent overcommit while efficiently utilizing nodes.Balancing multiple resource types is computationally complex.Affinity, anti-affinity, role, and region constraints may conflict.Deterministic conflict resolution is needed to avoid unpredictable placement.Network Locality & LatencyLatency-sensitive services require co-location or minimum cross-region communication.Must apply network penalties while still finding feasible nodes.Concurrency & Race ConditionsMultiple containers may be scheduled simultaneously.Atomic reservation of resources is required to prevent oversubscription.If no node satisfies all constraints, the system must retry intelligently or preempt lower-priority containers.How to optimize placement for mixed workloads (latency-sensitive + batch jobs)?How to score nodes when multiple constraints conflict?How often should node metrics be updated to ensure accurate scoring?Should node selection consider historical performance or only current state?How to prevent cascading placement failures when many containers are scheduled at once?Health monitoring is critical for ensuring container reliability, detecting failures early, and triggering recovery mechanisms. It goes beyond simple “is container running?” checks; it involves continuous observation of liveness, readiness, resource usage, latency, and error trends, forming the basis for automated recovery, scaling, and performance optimization.Liveness & Readiness Detection Detect if containers are alive (running) and ready (able to serve requests). Failure to detect unhealthy containers promptly can cause downtime or degraded performance. Containers may fail intermittently; probes must handle transient failures without triggering false positives.Resource & Latency Metrics Collection Track CPU, memory, network usage, request latency, and I/O metrics in real time. Metrics are used for scaling decisions, bottleneck detection, and failure diagnosis.High-frequency metric collection can be resource-intensive.Metrics must be normalized and aggregated across containers, nodes, and regions.Error Tracking & Degradation Scoring Quantify service degradation over time to decide on restarts or replacements. Intermittent errors or slow responses must be distinguished from persistent failures.Requires scoring over time windows.Must correlate multiple metrics (latency, CPU spikes, error rates) to detect genuine degradation.Automatic Failure Management Trigger container restarts, replacements, or recovery mechanisms when failures are detected. Ensures high availability and resilience.Multiple failures may happen simultaneously.Stateful services require special handling (leader election, volume recovery).Recovery must be deterministic to avoid cascading failures.Leader Election & Stateful Recovery For stateful services, ensure correct leader election and volume recovery. Stateful services cannot tolerate inconsistent leader assignment or data loss.Distributed leader election protocols must converge quickly.Volume recovery may involve waiting for persistent storage synchronization.Implement liveness and readiness probes with retry mechanisms.Collect high-frequency CPU, memory, network, and latency metrics.Aggregate metrics over containers, nodes, and services.Compute error scores and detect degradation trends.Trigger FailureManager actions: restart, replace, or reschedule containers.Manage stateful services: leader election, volume recovery, and failover.Handle transient failures vs persistent failures deterministically.Avoid false positives that could trigger unnecessary restarts.How often should probes run to balance detection speed and system load?How to distinguish transient failures from genuine failures?How to aggregate metrics efficiently across clusters?When should error scores trigger automated recovery?How to safely recover stateful containers without data loss?How to handle cascading failures across nodes or services?Health Monitoring Overview ┌─────────────────────┐ │ Start Health Monitor│ │ ┌─────────────────────────┐ │ Liveness & Readiness? │ └─────────┬──────────────┘ ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐ ▼ ▼ Healthy Unhealthy │ │ ▼ ▼ Collect Metrics Retry Probe (n times) │ │ ▼ ▼ Aggregate Metrics Still Unhealthy? │ │ ▼ ▼ Compute Error Score Trigger FailureManager │ │ ▼ ▼ Check Degradation Threshold Restart / Replace │ │ ▼ ▼ Stateful Service? Leader Election / \ │ │Volume Recovery Log & Continuefunction monitorContainer(container): while container.isRunning(): if not checkLiveness(container): retryLiveness(container, retries=3, backoff=True) if not checkReadiness(container): retryReadiness(container, retries=3, backoff=True) metrics = collectMetrics(container) aggregateMetrics(container.service, metrics) errorScore = computeErrorScore(container, metrics) if errorScore > threshold: triggerFailureManager(container) # Special handling for stateful containers if container.isStateful(): ensureLeaderElection(container.service) recoverVolumes(container) ┌─────────────────────┐ │ Start Monitor Loop │ │ Check Liveness & Readiness ┌─────────────┴─────────────┐ ▼ ▼ Healthy Unhealthy │ │ ▼ ▼ Collect Metrics Retry Probes │ │ ▼ ▼ Aggregate & Compute Error Score Still Unhealthy? │ │ ▼ ▼Threshold Exceeded? Trigger FailureManager │ │ Yes ──┴── No └───────┐ │ │ Stateful Service? Restart/Replace / \ │ │Volume Recovery Log & Continuefunction triggerFailureManager(container): if container.isStateless(): createReplacement(container.service) # Stateful recovery recoverVolumes(container) createReplacement(container.service) ensureLeaderElection(container.service) log("Failure handled for container:", container.id)Scoring & Degradation Function Examplefunction computeErrorScore(container, metrics): score = 0 if metrics.cpu > container.cpuLimit: score += 2 if metrics.memory > container.memoryLimit: score += 2 if metrics.latency > container.latencyThreshold: score += 1 if metrics.errorRate > container.errorThreshold: score += 3Critical Points & ChallengesTransient vs Persistent FailuresProbes must distinguish between short-lived spikes and true failures to avoid unnecessary restarts.High-Frequency Metrics CollectionCollecting CPU, memory, network, and latency metrics across large clusters can be resource-intensive.Must correlate multiple metrics over time windows to detect degradation reliably.Stateful Service RecoveryLeader election and volume recovery must be deterministic to prevent data loss or split-brain scenarios.Health checks and FailureManager must coordinate to prevent mass restarts that could destabilize the cluster.The is the central system responsible for detecting, responding to, and escalating container failures. Its primary goal is self-healing and resilience, ensuring that both transient and persistent failures are handled safely without compromising cluster stability.It coordinates with health checks, the registry, lifecycle management, and placement logic to make deterministic recovery decisions.Concurrent Failure Handling Multiple managers or schedulers may attempt to recover the same container simultaneously. Without coordination, duplicate restarts or replacements can occur, leading to inconsistencies. Requires distributed locking or consensus mechanisms in a large cluster. Containers may fail temporarily due to spikes or transient errors. Restarting aggressively can worsen instability. Soft restarts (graceful retries) help recover transient failures without unnecessary replacements. Must determine thresholds for retries, backoffs, and escalation to replacement.Persistent Failure Tracking Identify containers that repeatedly fail and escalate them. Persistent failures often indicate underlying system issues, configuration problems, or code defects. Requires maintaining historical failure data and correlating failures across time. Some failures are critical and cannot be handled automatically; human intervention is required. Escalation prevents unnoticed systemic issues and ensures SLA adherence. Must distinguish between recoverable and critical failures and trigger timely alerts without flooding operators.Cluster-Safe Self-Healing Ensure recovery actions do not destabilize the cluster, especially for high-priority or stateful services. Incorrect handling can lead to cascading failures. Requires coordination with placement logic, health checks, and stateful recovery mechanisms.Acquire distributed lock (KV lock) to ensure exclusive handling of a container.Decide whether to perform a soft restart or replacement.Track failure counts and calculate persistent failure scores.Trigger alerts or rollback if failure exceeds thresholds.Integrate with stateful service recovery (leader election, volume recovery).Coordinate recovery actions with lifecycle manager to avoid duplicate efforts.Handle cascading failures without destabilizing the cluster.How to safely acquire and release distributed locks for failure handling?How many retries should a soft restart perform before escalating to replacement?How to track and score persistent failures across multiple nodes or time windows?When should human intervention be triggered versus automatic recovery?How to prevent cascading restarts when multiple containers fail simultaneously? ┌─────────────────────┐ │ Container Failure? │ │ Acquire KV Lock? Yes No │ │ ▼ ▼ Soft Restart Possible? Wait & Retry / \ │ │ Perform Soft Restart Replace Container ▼ ▼ Failure Resolved?
